title: A1C to Average Glucose Calculator (eAG)
shortDescription: Convert your HbA1c percentage into estimated average glucose in mg/dL and mmol/L.
description: This calculator turns an HbA1c (%) value into estimated average glucose (eAG), showing both mg/dL and mmol/L. It uses the standard formula to help you understand what your A1C number means in everyday blood sugar terms for diabetes management.
metaDescription: Free A1C to average glucose calculator. Enter your HbA1c percentage to estimate your average blood glucose in both mg/dL and mmol/L instantly.

result.label: Estimated average glucose
result.empty: Enter your HbA1c percentage to see your estimated average glucose.

sections.heading: What is estimated average glucose (eAG)?
paragraphs: HbA1c reflects your average blood sugar over the past two to three months. Estimated average glucose (eAG) translates that percentage into the same units you see on a daily glucose meter.
The conversion uses the formula mg/dL = 28.7 × A1C − 46.7, and mmol/L = mg/dL ÷ 18, so you can compare your lab result to everyday readings.

sections.heading: Why eAG is useful
paragraphs: Seeing your A1C as an average glucose number makes the result easier to relate to. A higher A1C means a higher average glucose and generally a higher risk of diabetes complications.
Use eAG as a reference point alongside your meter readings, not as a replacement for them or for professional guidance.

howTo.name: Enter your HbA1c
howTo.text: Type your HbA1c value as a percentage, for example 7.0, into the HbA1c (%) field.

howTo.name: Read your eAG
howTo.text: The calculator instantly shows your estimated average glucose in mg/dL and mmol/L.

howTo.name: Compare and discuss
howTo.text: Compare the result with your meter readings and discuss your targets with your clinician.

faq.q: How is A1C converted to average glucose?
faq.a: The calculator uses mg/dL = 28.7 × A1C − 46.7, then divides mg/dL by 18 to get mmol/L.

faq.q: Is this result a diagnosis or medical advice?
faq.a: No. This is only an estimate and not medical advice. Always consult a qualified clinician about your results and treatment.

faq.q: Why does eAG differ from my meter readings?
faq.a: eAG is an average over months, while meter readings show single moments. Day-to-day values naturally rise and fall around the average.
An A1C below 5.7% is generally considered normal, but targets vary by person. Ask your clinician what range is right for you.

faq.q: Can I enter A1C in mmol/mol?
faq.a: This tool expects A1C as a percentage. If your lab reports mmol/mol, convert it to a percentage first or ask your clinician.
